preamble.summary: One of the purest lament psalms in the Psalter — six verses that move through three distinct emotional stages with architectural precision. The psalmist asks 'How long?' four times (vv. 2-3), pleads for God to look and answer (vv. 4-5), then erupts into trust and praise (v. 6). The entire emotional arc of the lament genre — distress, petition, confidence — is compressed into the smallest possible space.
preamble.remarkable: The fourfold 'How long?' (ad anah) in verses 2-3 is one of the most imitated structures in the history of prayer. Each question targets a different dimension of suffering: How long will you forget me? (divine absence), How long will you hide your face? (divine concealment), How long must I wrestle with my thoughts? (internal anguish), How long will my enemy triumph? (external threat). The questions spiral from God to self to enemy, mapping the complete landscape of despair. Then — without any reported answer from God, without any change in circumstances — the psalm pivots to trust in verse 6. This pivot is the mystery at the heart of biblical lament: the act of crying out to God is itself the bridge from despair to praise. Nothing changes except the direction of the psalmist's gaze.
preamble.friction: The transition from verse 5 ('lest my enemy say, I have prevailed') to verse 6 ('But I trust in your faithful love') is one of the sharpest turns in the Psalter. There is no intervening oracle, no divine response, no change in situation. Some scholars attribute this to a liturgical pause — perhaps a priest spoke an oracle of assurance between verses 5 and 6 that the text does not record. Others see it as the essential structure of faith: lament that is directed toward God already contains the seeds of trust, because the act of addressing God presupposes his existence, his power, and his willingness to hear.
preamble.connections: The fourfold 'How long?' echoes Habakkuk 1:2 and Revelation 6:10, forming a thread that runs from the Psalms through the prophets to the apocalypse. The final verse's movement from trust (batachti) to rejoicing (yagel libbi) to singing (ashirah) anticipates the full trajectory of Psalm 30, which moves from mourning to dancing. The phrase 'he has dealt generously with me' (gamal alay) will reappear in Psalm 116:7, connecting this brief lament to the great thanksgiving psalms.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: The superscription is minimal — no musical or situational notation beyond the standard attribution to David and the choirmaster. The simplicity of the heading matches the compressed intensity of the psalm itself.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־אָ֣נָה יְ֭הוָה תִּשְׁכָּחֵ֣נִי נֶ֑צַח עַד־אָ֓נָה ׀ תַּסְתִּ֖יר אֶת־פָּנֶ֣יךָ מִמֶּֽנִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: How long wilt thou forget me, O LORD? for ever? how long wilt thou hide thy face from me?
verses.rendering:
How long, LORD? Will you forget me forever?
How long will you hide your face from me?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm plunges immediately into the first two of four 'How long?' (ad anah) questions. The first targets divine memory: tishkacheni netsach ('will you forget me perpetually?'). The word netsach ('forever, perpetually, to the end') transforms the question from a complaint about duration into an existential crisis — is God's forgetting permanent? The second question escalates: tastir et panekha mimmenni ('will you hide your face from me?'). The hiding of God's face (hester panim) is the most severe form of divine judgment in the Hebrew Bible — not active punishment but withdrawal, absence, the turning away of the gaze that sustains life. In Deuteronomy 31:17-18, God warns that he will hide his face as a consequence of Israel's unfaithfulness. The psalmist experiences this hiddenness not as punishment for sin but as inexplicable abandonment.
translator_notes: The phrase hester panim ('hiding of the face') is a technical theological concept. When God turns his face toward someone, it means blessing and life (Numbers 6:25). When he hides his face, it means exposure to chaos and death. The psalmist is asking: have you turned away permanently?

key_terms.semantic_range: how long, until when, to what point
key_terms.note: ad anah is the characteristic cry of the biblical lament. It appears in Psalms 6, 74, 79, 80, 89, and 94, as well as in the prophets. It does not question God's existence but his timing — it presupposes that God can and will act, and demands to know when.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־אָ֨נָה אָשִׁ֪ית עֵצ֡וֹת בְּנַפְשִׁ֗י יָג֣וֹן בִּלְבָבִ֣י יוֹמָ֑ם עַד־אָ֓נָה ׀ יָר֖וּם אֹיְבִ֣י עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: How long shall I take counsel in my soul, having sorrow in my heart daily? how long shall mine enemy be exalted over me?
verses.rendering:
How long must I wrestle with my thoughts,
with grief in my heart day after day?
How long will my enemy be exalted over me?
verses.expanded_rendering: The third and fourth questions turn from God to the psalmist's own experience. The phrase ashit etsot be-nafshi ('I place counsels in my soul') describes the exhausting internal process of trying to think a way out — turning the problem over and over, seeking plans and strategies that lead nowhere. The noun etsah ('counsel, plan') in the plural suggests multiple strategies attempted and abandoned. Meanwhile, grief (yagon) has taken up residence in his heart (be-levavi) not occasionally but yomam ('daily, by day'). The fourth question names the external enemy: ad anah yarum oyevi alay ('how long will my enemy be raised over me?'). The verb rum ('to be high, exalted') suggests dominance — the enemy is above, the psalmist is below. The four questions together map a complete topography of suffering: God is absent, the self is anguished, the enemy is triumphant.
translator_notes: The fourfold repetition of ad anah creates a hammering rhythm in Hebrew that formal English translation struggles to reproduce. The questions are not rhetorical — they demand an answer. Their repetition is itself a form of prayer: the persistence of the question is the persistence of faith.

verses.text_hebrew: הַבִּ֣יטָה עֲ֭נֵנִי יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהָ֑י הָאִ֥ירָה עֵ֝ינַ֗י פֶּן־אִישַׁ֥ן הַמָּֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Consider and hear me, O LORD my God: lighten mine eyes, lest I sleep the sleep of death;
verses.rendering:
Look at me! Answer me, LORD my God!
Give light to my eyes,
or I will sleep the sleep of death.
verses.expanded_rendering: The petition section opens with three imperatives in rapid succession: habbitah ('look!'), aneni ('answer me!'), ha'irah einay ('give light to my eyes!'). Each imperative counters one aspect of the suffering described in vv. 2-3. God has hidden his face — so the psalmist demands that God look. God has been silent — so the psalmist demands an answer. The psalmist is sinking toward death — so he asks God to illuminate his eyes. The phrase ha'irah einay ('give light to my eyes') refers to the brightness and clarity of the living eye; when life fades, the eyes grow dim (1 Samuel 14:27-29, Genesis 27:1). To have light in the eyes is to be alive and alert. The alternative is ishan hammavet ('I will sleep death') — death described as a sleep from which there is no waking. The petitions are arranged in escalating urgency: look, answer, or I die.
translator_notes: The title 'LORD my God' (YHVH Elohai) is significant — in the midst of feeling abandoned, the psalmist still claims God as his own. The possessive 'my' is an act of faith embedded in the petition. He addresses the God who seems absent as 'my God.'

verses.text_hebrew: פֶּן־יֹאמַ֣ר אֹיְבִ֣י יְכׇלְתִּ֑יו צָרַ֥י יָ֝גִ֗ילוּ כִּ֣י אֶמּֽוֹט׃
verses.text_kjv: Lest mine enemy say, I have prevailed against him; and those that trouble me rejoice when I am moved.
verses.rendering:
Do not let my enemy say, 'I have prevailed over him!'
Do not let my foes rejoice when I stumble.
translator_notes: The psalmist appeals to God's honor: if the enemy prevails, it reflects not only on the psalmist but on the God who was supposed to protect him. This appeal to divine reputation is a common strategy in biblical lament — Moses uses it in Numbers 14:13-16, arguing that the nations will interpret Israel's destruction as God's inability to save.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֤י ׀ בְּחַסְדְּךָ֣ בָטָחְתִּי֮ יָ֤גֵ֥ל לִבִּ֗י בִּ֫ישׁוּעָתֶ֥ךָ אָשִׁ֥ירָה לַיהוָ֑ה כִּ֖י גָמַ֣ל עָלָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But I have trusted in thy mercy; my heart shall rejoice in thy salvation. I will sing unto the LORD, because he hath dealt bountifully with me.
verses.rendering:
But I have trusted in your faithful love;
my heart will rejoice in your salvation.
I will sing to the LORD,
for he has dealt generously with me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The pivot word is va-ani ('but I') — a sharp adversative that sets the psalmist's stance against everything that preceded. Despite the absence, the grief, the enemy's triumph, the psalmist declares: be-chasdekha batachti ('in your faithful love I have trusted'). The verb batach ('to trust') is in the perfect tense — this trust is already established, not a future aspiration. The object of trust is chesed, God's covenant-faithful love, the most durable word in the Hebrew theological vocabulary. From trust the psalm moves to joy: yagel libbi bishu'atekha ('my heart will rejoice in your salvation'). The verb gil describes exuberant, leaping joy. Then to song: ashirah la-YHVH ('I will sing to the LORD'). The final clause — ki gamal alay ('for he has dealt generously with me') — speaks in the past tense as though the deliverance has already occurred. This is the language of anticipatory faith: the psalmist sings of God's generosity before seeing it, trusting that the faithful love he has banked on will prove reliable.
translator_notes: The movement from lament (vv. 2-3) through petition (vv. 4-5) to praise (v. 6) is the classic three-part structure of the biblical lament. What makes Psalm 13 remarkable is that this entire arc occurs in six verses. The compression intensifies every transition.

The verb gamal ('to deal with, to repay, to wean') implies completion — God has brought something to its full development. The psalmist trusts that God's dealing with him will end in generosity, not abandonment.

key_terms.semantic_range: loyal love, covenant faithfulness, steadfast love, kindness, devotion
key_terms.note: chesed is the gravitational center of Israel's theology. It describes God's commitment to his covenant partners that persists even when those partners falter. Here it is the one thing the psalmist trusts when everything else has failed — not God's power, not God's justice, but his faithful love.

yeshu'ah derives from the root yasha ('to save, deliver') and can refer to military victory, rescue from danger, or divine deliverance from any threat. It is the noun form of the concept embedded in the names Joshua and Jesus.
